html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Fifteen — Part Four

The Book of Melakhim Bet

MELAKHIM BET

The Second Book of Kings

Chapters 20 through 25

Khizkiyahu's Illness — Menasheh — Yoshiyahu's Reformation — The Fall of Yerushalayim

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

No complete DSS manuscript for Melakhim Bet

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

MELAKHIM BET — CHAPTERS 20 THROUGH 25 — YOSHIYAHU TO THE FALL OF YERUSHALAYIM

MELAKHIM BET — CHAPTER 20 Khizkiyahu's Illness — The Sun Goes Back — The Babylonian Envoys — All Shall Be Carried to Babylon

1 In those days Khizkiyahu became sick and was at the point of death. And Yesha'yahu the prophet the son of Amoz came to him and said to him, Thus says YHWH, Set your house in order, for you shall die — you shall not recover from this illness.

2 Then Khizkiyahu turned his face to the wall and prayed to YHWH, saying,

3 Now, O YHWH, please remember how I have walked before you in faithfulness and with a whole heart, and have done what is good in your sight. And Khizkiyahu wept bitterly.

4 And before Yesha'yahu had gone out of the middle court, the word of YHWH came to him:

5 Turn back, and say to Khizkiyahu the leader of my people, Thus says YHWH, the Creator of Dawid your father, I have heard your prayer — I have seen your tears. Behold, I will heal you. On the third day you shall go up to the house of YHWH.

6 And I will add fifteen years to your life. I will deliver you and this city out of the hand of the king of Assyria, and I will defend this city for my own sake and for my servant Dawid's sake.

7 And Yesha'yahu said, Bring a cake of figs. And let them take and lay it on the boil, that he may recover.

8 And Khizkiyahu said to Yesha'yahu, What shall be the sign that YHWH will heal me, and that I shall go up to the house of YHWH on the third day?

9 And Yesha'yahu said, This shall be the sign to you from YHWH, that YHWH will do the thing that he has promised: shall the shadow go forward ten steps, or go back ten steps?

10 And Khizkiyahu answered, It is an easy thing for the shadow to lengthen ten steps. Rather let the shadow go back ten steps.

11 And Yesha'yahu the prophet called to YHWH, and he brought the shadow back ten steps, by which it had gone down on the steps of Akhaz.

12 At that time Merodakh-Baladan the son of Baladan, king of Babylon, sent envoys with letters and a present to Khizkiyahu, for he heard that Khizkiyahu had been sick.

13 And Khizkiyahu welcomed them, and he showed them all his treasure house, the silver, the gold, the spices, the precious oil, his armory, all that was found in his storehouses. There was nothing in his house or in all his realm that Khizkiyahu did not show them.

14 Then Yesha'yahu the prophet came to King Khizkiyahu, and said to him, What did these men say? And from where did they come to you? Khizkiyahu said, They have come from a far country, from Babylon.

15 He said, What have they seen in your house? And Khizkiyahu answered, They have seen all that is in my house — there is nothing in my storehouses that I did not show them.

16 Then Yesha'yahu said to Khizkiyahu, Hear the word of YHWH:

17 Behold, the days are coming, when all that is in your house, and that which your fathers have stored up till this day, shall be carried to Babylon. Nothing shall be left, says YHWH.

18 And some of your own sons, who will come from you, whom you will father, shall be taken away, and they shall be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon.

19 Then Khizkiyahu said to Yesha'yahu, The word of YHWH that you have spoken is good. For he thought, Why not, if there will be peace and security in my days?

20 The rest of the deeds of Khizkiyahu and all his might, and how he made the pool and the conduit and brought water into the city, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Yehudah?

21 And Khizkiyahu slept with his fathers, and Menasheh his son reigned in his place.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Bet survives. Chapter 20 contains the covenant reversal of Khizkiyahu's death sentence — YHWH extends his life fifteen years — and the covenant tragedy of the Babylonian envoys. The covenant king who prayed so effectively against Sanheriv shows all his treasures to Babylon's representative, planting the seed of the future exile.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 20 in full. Verse 3 — please remember how I have walked before you in faithfulness and with a whole heart — Khizkiyahu's prayer is covenant biography: he does not appeal to general mercy but to the specific record of his covenant life. YHWH responds by adding fifteen years.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 19 — the word of YHWH that you have spoken is good — for there will be peace and security in my days. Khizkiyahu's response to the prophecy of the Babylonian exile is the most troubling statement attributed to him: relief that the disaster will not come in his own lifetime. The greatest covenant king of Yehudah reveals here a failure of covenant solidarity with the generations that follow.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13 — Khizkiyahu showed them all his treasure house — there was nothing he did not show them. The same king who spread Sanheriv's threatening letter before YHWH now opens every chamber to Babylon's envoys. The covenant wisdom that characterized his response to the Assyrian crisis is absent here. Yesha'yahu's question — what did they see? — produces the answer that triggers the covenant prophecy: everything. The Babylonian exile is announced not as a consequence of idolatry but as the consequence of a king showing his treasures to an envoy from Babylon. Covenant pride in achievement — displayed without discernment — plants the seed of destruction.

MELAKHIM BET — CHAPTER 21 Menasheh — Worse Than the Amorites — He Shed Very Much Innocent Blood — Amon Assassinated

1 Menasheh was twelve years old when he began to reign, and he reigned fifty-five years in Yerushalayim. His mother's name was Kheftzivah.

2 And he did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, according to the despicable practices of the nations whom YHWH drove out before the people of Yisra'EL.

3 For he rebuilt the high places that Khizkiyahu his father had destroyed, and he erected altars for Ba'al and made an Asherah, as Akhav king of Yisra'EL had done, and worshiped all the host of heaven and served them.

4 And he built altars in the house of YHWH, of which YHWH had said, In Yerushalayim will I put my name.

5 And he built altars for all the host of heaven in the two courts of the house of YHWH.

6 And he burned his son as an offering and used fortune-telling and omens and dealt with mediums and with necromancers. He did much evil in the sight of YHWH, provoking him to anger.

7 And the carved image of Asherah that he had made he set in the house of which YHWH said to Dawid and to Shelomoh his son, In this house, and in Yerushalayim, which I have chosen out of all the tribes of Yisra'EL, I will put my name forever.

8 And I will not cause the feet of Yisra'EL to wander anymore out of the land that I gave to their fathers, if only they will be careful to do according to all that I have commanded them, and according to all the Torah that my servant Moshe commanded them.

9 But they did not listen, and Menasheh led them astray to do more evil than the nations had done whom YHWH destroyed before the people of Yisra'EL.

10 And YHWH said by his servants the prophets,

11 Because Menasheh king of Yehudah has committed these abominations and has done things more evil than all that the Amorites did, who were before him, and has made Yehudah also to sin with his idols,

12 Therefore thus says YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, Behold, I am bringing upon Yerushalayim and Yehudah such disaster that the ears of everyone who hears of it will tingle.

13 And I will stretch over Yerushalayim the measuring line of Samaria, and the plumb line of the house of Akhav, and I will wipe Yerushalayim as one wipes a dish, wiping it and turning it upside down.

14 And I will forsake the remnant of my heritage and give them into the hand of their enemies, and they shall become a prey and a spoil to all their enemies,

15 Because they have done what is evil in my sight and have provoked me to anger, since the day their fathers came out of Egypt, even to this day.

16 Moreover, Menasheh shed very much innocent blood, till he had filled Yerushalayim from one end to another, besides the sin that he made Yehudah to sin so that they did what was evil in the sight of YHWH.

17 Now the rest of the acts of Menasheh and all that he did, and the sin that he committed, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Yehudah?

18 And Menasheh slept with his fathers and was buried in the garden of his house, in the garden of Uzza. And Amon his son reigned in his place.

19 Amon was twenty-two years old when he began to reign, and he reigned two years in Yerushalayim. His mother's name was Meshullemet the daughter of Kharuz of Yotvah.

20 And he did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, as Menasheh his father had done.

21 He walked in all the way that his father walked and served the idols that his father served and worshiped them.

22 He abandoned YHWH, the Creator of his fathers, and did not walk in the way of YHWH.

23 And the servants of Amon conspired against him and put the king to death in his house.

24 But the people of the land struck down all those who had conspired against King Amon, and the people of the land made Yoshiyahu his son king in his place.

25 Now the rest of the acts of Amon that he did, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Yehudah?

26 And he was buried in his tomb in the garden of Uzza. And Yoshiyahu his son reigned in his place.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Bet survives. Menasheh's fifty-five year reign — the longest in the entire biblical record — is the covenant catastrophe that seals the fate of Yehudah. Despite Yoshiyahu's subsequent reformation, verse 13 declares the disaster already determined because of Menasheh. The covenant judgment against Menasheh is irrevocable.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 21 in full. Verse 16 — Menasheh shed very much innocent blood, till he had filled Yerushalayim from one end to another — is read as the covenant indictment that goes beyond religious apostasy to the shedding of innocent blood, the most fundamental covenant violation. The prophetic tradition identifies Yesha'yahu among Menasheh's victims.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 13 — I will wipe Yerushalayim as one wipes a dish, wiping it and turning it upside down — is the most graphic covenant image of destruction in Melakhim. The city built for YHWH's name will be treated like a used dish — wiped clean and inverted.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — Menasheh placed a carved image of Asherah in the house of which YHWH said, In this house I will put my name forever. The covenant outrage is precise: the idol occupies the space YHWH declared as the place of his name. Verse 9 — Menasheh led them astray to do more evil than the nations had done whom YHWH destroyed before Yisra'EL. The standard against which covenant Israel is measured is the Canaanite nations. Menasheh exceeded them. The son of the greatest covenant king of Yehudah produced the worst covenant apostasy — fifty-five years of it. Verse 11-12 — YHWH declares the disaster determined. Yoshiyahu's reformation will delay it but not prevent it.

YOSHIYAHU'S REFORMATION AND THE FALL OF YERUSHALAYIM — CHAPTERS 22 THROUGH 25

MELAKHIM BET — CHAPTER 22 Yoshiyahu's Covenant Reformation — The Book of the Torah Found — Khuldah the Prophetess Consulted

1 Yoshiyahu was eight years old when he began to reign, and he reigned thirty-one years in Yerushalayim. His mother's name was Yedidah the daughter of Adayah of Botzkat.

2 And he did what was right in the eyes of YHWH and walked in all the way of Dawid his father, and he did not turn aside to the right or to the left.

3 In the eighteenth year of King Yoshiyahu, the king sent Shafan the son of Atzalyahu, son of Meshullam, the secretary, to the house of YHWH, saying,

4 Go up to Khilkiyahu the high priest, that he may count the money that has been brought into the house of YHWH, which the gatekeepers have collected from the people.

5 And let it be given into the hand of the workmen who have the oversight of the house of YHWH, and let them give it to the workmen who are at the house of YHWH, repairing the house,

6 That is, to the carpenters, and to the builders, and to the masons, as well as for buying timber and quarried stone to repair the house.

7 But no accounting shall be asked from them for the money that is delivered into their hand, for they deal honestly.

8 And Khilkiyahu the high priest said to Shafan the secretary, I have found the Book of the Torah in the house of YHWH. And Khilkiyahu gave the book to Shafan, and he read it.

9 And Shafan the secretary came to the king, and reported to the king, Your servants have emptied out the money that was found in the house and have delivered it into the hand of the workmen who have the oversight of the house of YHWH.

10 Then Shafan the secretary told the king, Khilkiyahu the priest has given me a book. And Shafan read it before the king.

11 When the king heard the words of the Book of the Torah, he tore his clothes.

12 And the king commanded Khilkiyahu the priest, and Akhikam the son of Shafan, and Akhbor the son of Mikhayahu, and Shafan the secretary, and Asayah the king's servant, saying,

13 Go, inquire of YHWH for me, and for the people, and for all Yehudah, concerning the words of this book that has been found. For great is the wrath of YHWH that is kindled against us, because our fathers have not obeyed the words of this book, to do according to all that is written concerning us.

14 So Khilkiyahu the priest, and Akhikam, and Akhbor, and Shafan, and Asayah went to Khuldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the son of Tikva, son of Kharhas, keeper of the wardrobe — now she lived in Yerushalayim in the Second Quarter — and they talked with her.

15 And she said to them, Thus says YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL: Tell the man who sent you to me,

16 Thus says YHWH, Behold, I will bring disaster upon this place and upon its inhabitants, all the words of the book that the king of Yehudah has read.

17 Because they have forsaken me and have made offerings to other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the work of their hands, therefore my wrath will be kindled against this place, and it will not be quenched.

18 But to the king of Yehudah, who sent you to inquire of YHWH, thus shall you say to him, Thus says YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL: Regarding the words that you have heard,

19 Because your heart was penitent, and you humbled yourself before YHWH, when you heard how I spoke against this place and against its inhabitants, that they should become a desolation and a curse, and you have torn your clothes and wept before me, I also have heard you, declares YHWH.

20 Therefore, behold, I will gather you to your fathers, and you shall be gathered to your grave in peace, and your eyes shall not see all the disaster that I will bring upon this place. And they brought back word to the king.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Bet survives. The finding of the Book of the Torah in the temple — verse 8 — is one of the most significant events in the entire Nevi'im. Most covenant scholarship identifies this as a form of Devarim. The book had been lost — or suppressed — during Menasheh's reign. Its rediscovery in the temple triggers the most comprehensive covenant reformation in Yehudah's history.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 22 in full. Khuldah the prophetess — verse 14 — is one of five women explicitly named as prophets in the covenant canon. The king sends the high priest and the secretary to inquire of YHWH, and YHWH's answer comes through a woman. The covenant principle: YHWH speaks through whomever he chooses.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 11 — when the king heard the words of the Book of the Torah, he tore his clothes. The covenant document that should have governed the entire monarchy from Shelomoh onward has been absent so long that when a king finally hears it, he tears his clothes in grief at how far the covenant community has strayed.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13 — great is the wrath of YHWH that is kindled against us, because our fathers have not obeyed the words of this book. Yoshiyahu's response to the Torah's rediscovery is not pride in his own reformation but grief at the covenant failure of his fathers. He does not say YHWH will be pleased with my reforms — he says the wrath is already kindled and we need to know the full extent of it. Verse 19-20 — because your heart was penitent — I will gather you to your grave in peace — your eyes shall not see all the disaster. Khuldah's covenant word confirms that the disaster is coming — it simply will not come in Yoshiyahu's days. The reformation delays the judgment without canceling it. Covenant repentance receives covenant mercy without covenant accountability being removed.

MELAKHIM BET — CHAPTER 23 The Covenant Renewed — The Great Reformation — Yoshiyahu Purges All Idolatry — Yoshiyahu Dies at Megiddo

1 Then the king sent, and all the elders of Yehudah and Yerushalayim were gathered to him.

2 And the king went up to the house of YHWH, and with him all the men of Yehudah and all the inhabitants of Yerushalayim and the priests and the prophets, all the people, both small and great. And he read in their hearing all the words of the Book of the Covenant that had been found in the house of YHWH.

3 And the king stood by the pillar and made a covenant before YHWH, to walk after YHWH and to keep his commandments and his testimonies and his statutes with all his heart and all his soul, to perform the words of this covenant that were written in this book. And all the people joined in the covenant.

4 And the king commanded Khilkiyahu the high priest and the priests of the second order and the keepers of the threshold to bring out of the temple of YHWH all the vessels made for Ba'al, for Asherah, and for all the host of heaven. He burned them outside Yerushalayim in the fields of the Kidron and carried their ashes to Beit-El.

5 And he deposed the priests whom the kings of Yehudah had ordained to make offerings in the high places at the cities of Yehudah and around Yerushalayim — those also who burned incense to Ba'al, to the sun and the moon and the constellations and all the host of the heavens.

6 And he brought out the Asherah from the house of YHWH, outside Yerushalayim, to the brook Kidron, and burned it at the brook Kidron and beat it to dust and cast the dust of it upon the graves of the common people.

7 And he broke down the houses of the male cult prostitutes that were in the house of YHWH, where the women wove hangings for the Asherah.

8 And he brought all the priests out of the cities of Yehudah, and defiled the high places where the priests had made offerings, from Geva to Be'er-Sheva. And he broke down the high places of the gates that were at the entrance of the gate of Yehoshua the governor of the city, which were on one's left at the gate of the city.

9 However, the priests of the high places did not come up to the altar of YHWH in Yerushalayim, but they ate unleavened bread among their brothers.

10 And he defiled Tofet, which is in the Valley of the Son of Hinnom, that no one might burn his son or his daughter as an offering to Molekh.

11 And he removed the horses that the kings of Yehudah had dedicated to the sun, at the entrance to the house of YHWH, by the chamber of Nathan-Melekh the chamberlain, which was in the precincts. And he burned the chariots of the sun with fire.

12 And the altars on the roof of the upper chamber of Akhaz, which the kings of Yehudah had made, and the altars that Menasheh had made in the two courts of the house of YHWH, he pulled down and broke in pieces and cast the dust of them into the brook Kidron.

13 And the king defiled the high places that were east of Yerushalayim, to the south of the Mount of Corruption, which Shelomoh the king of Yisra'EL had built for Ashtoreth the abomination of the Tzidonians, and for Kemosh the abomination of Moav, and for Milkom the abomination of the Ammonites.

14 And he broke in pieces the pillars and cut down the Asherim and filled their places with the bones of men.

15 Moreover, the altar at Beit-El, the high place erected by Yarovam the son of Nevat, who made Yisra'EL to sin, that altar also and the high place he pulled down, and he burned the high place, crushing it to dust. He also burned the Asherah.

16 And as Yoshiyahu turned, he saw the tombs there on the mount. And he sent and took the bones out of the tombs and burned them on the altar and defiled it, according to the word of YHWH that the man of Creator proclaimed, who had predicted these things.

17 Then he said, What is that monument that I see? And the men of the city told him, It is the tomb of the man of Creator who came from Yehudah and predicted these things that you have done against the altar at Beit-El.

18 And he said, Let him be — let no man move his bones. So they let his bones alone, with the bones of the prophet who came out of Samaria.

19 And Yoshiyahu also removed all the shrines of the high places that were in the cities of Samaria, which kings of Yisra'EL had made, provoking YHWH to anger. He did to them according to all that he had done at Beit-El.

20 And he sacrificed all the priests of the high places who were there, on the altars, and burned human bones on them. Then he returned to Yerushalayim.

21 And the king commanded all the people, Keep the Passover to YHWH your Creator, as it is written in this Book of the Covenant.

22 For no such Passover had been kept since the days of the judges who judged Yisra'EL, or during all the days of the kings of Yisra'EL or of the kings of Yehudah.

23 But in the eighteenth year of King Yoshiyahu this Passover was kept to YHWH in Yerushalayim.

24 Moreover, Yoshiyahu put away the mediums and the necromancers and the household gods and the idols and all the abominations that were seen in the land of Yehudah and in Yerushalayim, that he might establish the words of the Torah that were written in the book that Khilkiyahu the priest found in the house of YHWH.

25 Before him there was no king like him, who turned to YHWH with all his heart and with all his soul and with all his might, according to all the Torah of Moshe. Nor did any like him arise after him.

26 Still YHWH did not turn from the burning of his great wrath, by which his anger was kindled against Yehudah, because of all the provocations with which Menasheh had provoked him.

27 And YHWH said, I will remove Yehudah also out of my sight, as I have removed Yisra'EL, and I will cast off this city that I have chosen, Yerushalayim, and the house of which I said, My name shall be there.

28 Now the rest of the acts of Yoshiyahu and all that he did, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Yehudah?

29 In his days Pharaoh Nekho king of Egypt went up to the king of Assyria to the river Euphrates. King Yoshiyahu went to meet him, and Pharaoh Nekho killed him at Megiddo, as soon as he saw him.

30 And his servants carried him dead in a chariot from Megiddo and brought him to Yerushalayim and buried him in his own tomb. And the people of the land took Yeho'akhaz the son of Yoshiyahu, and anointed him, and made him king in his father's place.

31 Yeho'akhaz was twenty-three years old when he began to reign, and he reigned three months in Yerushalayim. His mother's name was Khamutal the daughter of Yirmeyahu of Livnah.

32 And he did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, according to all that his fathers had done.

33 And Pharaoh Nekho put him in bonds at Rivlah in the land of Khamath, so that he might not reign in Yerushalayim, and laid on the land a tribute of a hundred talents of silver and a talent of gold.

34 And Pharaoh Nekho made Elyakim the son of Yoshiyahu king in the place of Yoshiyahu his father, and changed his name to Yeho'yakim. But he took Yeho'akhaz away, and he came to Egypt and died there.

35 And Yeho'yakim gave the silver and the gold to Pharaoh, but he taxed the land to give the money according to the command of Pharaoh. He exacted the silver and the gold of the people of the land, from everyone according to his assessment, to give it to Pharaoh Nekho.

36 Yeho'yakim was twenty-five years old when he began to reign, and he reigned eleven years in Yerushalayim. His mother's name was Zevudah the daughter of Pedayah of Rumah.

37 And he did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, according to all that his fathers had done.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Bet survives. Chapter 23 records the most comprehensive covenant reformation in Yehudah's history — but verse 26 immediately declares that YHWH still did not turn from his great wrath because of Menasheh. The reformation of Yoshiyahu — the greatest in the entire Nevi'im — is insufficient to cancel the covenant judgment already determined.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 23 in full. Verse 25 — before him there was no king like him, who turned to YHWH with all his heart and with all his soul and with all his might, according to all the Torah of Moshe — is read as the highest covenant evaluation given to any king in the entire Nevi'im.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 16-17 — Yoshiyahu burns bones on the Beit-El altar, fulfilling the prophecy of the unnamed man of Creator in Melakhim Aleph 13:2 — spoken by name, three centuries before Yoshiyahu's birth. The covenant word reaches its mark exactly as declared.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 25-26 — the greatest possible covenant evaluation followed immediately by the irrevocable covenant judgment. Yoshiyahu is the best covenant king in the entire Nevi'im — and his reformation cannot cancel what Menasheh did over fifty-five years. The covenant principle is stark: personal faithfulness can delay and mitigate covenant judgment but cannot retroactively cancel the covenant violations of predecessors whose consequences are already in motion. Verse 29 — Yoshiyahu went to Megiddo to meet Pharaoh Nekho and was killed. The greatest covenant king of Yehudah dies in a battle he should not have entered, against an enemy YHWH had not sent him to fight. The Divrei HaYamim account records that Nekho warned Yoshiyahu: Creator has commanded me to hurry — cease opposing Creator, who is with me, lest he destroy you. Yoshiyahu did not listen.

MELAKHIM BET — CHAPTER 24 Nevukhadnetzar Besieges Yerushalayim — Yeho'yakim — Yeho'yakhin Surrenders — The First Deportation

1 In his days Nevukhadnetzar king of Babylon came up, and Yeho'yakim became his servant for three years. Then he turned and rebelled against him.

2 And YHWH sent against him bands of the Chaldeans and bands of the Arameans and bands of the Moavites and bands of the Ammonites, and sent them against Yehudah to destroy it, according to the word of YHWH that he spoke by his servants the prophets.

3 Surely this came upon Yehudah at the command of YHWH, to remove them out of his sight, for the sins of Menasheh, according to all that he had done,

4 And also for the innocent blood that he had shed. For he filled Yerushalayim with innocent blood, and YHWH would not pardon.

5 Now the rest of the deeds of Yeho'yakim and all that he did, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Yehudah?

6 So Yeho'yakim slept with his fathers, and Yeho'yakhin his son reigned in his place.

7 And the king of Egypt did not come again out of his land, for the king of Babylon had taken all that belonged to the king of Egypt from the Brook of Egypt to the river Euphrates.

8 Yeho'yakhin was eighteen years old when he became king, and he reigned three months in Yerushalayim. His mother's name was Nekhushta the daughter of Elnatan of Yerushalayim.

9 And he did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, according to all that his father had done.

10 At that time the servants of Nevukhadnetzar king of Babylon came up to Yerushalayim, and the city was besieged.

11 And Nevukhadnetzar king of Babylon came to the city while his servants were besieging it,

12 And Yeho'yakhin the king of Yehudah gave himself up to the king of Babylon, himself and his mother and his servants and his officials and his palace officials. The king of Babylon took him prisoner in the eighth year of his reign.

13 And he carried off all the treasures of the house of YHWH and the treasures of the king's house, and cut in pieces all the vessels of gold in the temple of YHWH, which Shelomoh king of Yisra'EL had made, as YHWH had foretold.

14 He carried away all Yerushalayim and all the officials and all the mighty men of valor, ten thousand captives, and all the craftsmen and the smiths. None remained, except the poorest people of the land.

15 And he carried away Yeho'yakhin to Babylon. The king's mother, the king's wives, his officials, and the chief men of the land he took into captivity from Yerushalayim to Babylon.

16 And the king of Babylon brought captive to Babylon all the men of valor, seven thousand, and the craftsmen and the metal workers, one thousand, all of them strong and fit for war.

17 And the king of Babylon made Mattanyahu, Yeho'yakhin's uncle, king in his place, and changed his name to Tzidkiyahu.

18 Tzidkiyahu was twenty-one years old when he became king, and he reigned eleven years in Yerushalayim. His mother's name was Khamutal the daughter of Yirmeyahu of Livnah.

19 And he did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, according to all that Yeho'yakim had done.

20 For because of the anger of YHWH it came to the point in Yerushalayim and Yehudah that he cast them out from his presence. And Tzidkiyahu rebelled against the king of Babylon.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Bet survives. Chapter 24 records the first deportation to Babylon — the best of the covenant community — the officials, warriors, craftsmen, and artisans — taken away while the poorest remain. Verse 2-4 — the covenant attribution: YHWH sent Nevukhadnetzar against Yehudah because of the sins of Menasheh, for the innocent blood he shed that YHWH would not pardon.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 24 in full. Verse 13 — he cut in pieces all the vessels of gold in the temple of YHWH, which Shelomoh king of Yisra'EL had made, as YHWH had foretold — is read as the covenant declaration that the destruction of the temple vessels was announced in advance by Yesha'yahu in Chapter 20:17.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 14 — none remained except the poorest people of the land. The covenant reversal of Khannah's song — he raises up the poor from the dust — is here enacted in its inverse: the great are carried away and the poor remain behind.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-4 — surely this came upon Yehudah at the command of YHWH — for the sins of Menasheh — and also for the innocent blood that he had shed — for he filled Yerushalayim with innocent blood, and YHWH would not pardon. The reason for the Babylonian exile is stated with covenant precision: Menasheh's sins — fifty-five years of them — and specifically the innocent blood he shed. YHWH would not pardon. The covenant declaration that some sins accumulate to the point where covenant patience reaches its limit — not because YHWH is unwilling to forgive individual repentance but because the covenant community's collective guilt has reached a threshold that justice requires answered.

MELAKHIM BET — CHAPTER 25 The Fall of Yerushalayim — The Temple Burned — Yehudah Carried to Babylon — Gedalyahu Killed — Yeho'yakhin Released

1 And in the ninth year of his reign, in the tenth month, on the tenth day of the month, Nevukhadnetzar king of Babylon came with all his army against Yerushalayim and laid siege to it. And they built siegeworks all around it.

2 So the city was besieged till the eleventh year of King Tzidkiyahu.

3 On the ninth day of the fourth month the famine was so severe in the city that there was no food for the people of the land.

4 Then a breach was made in the city, and all the men of war fled by night by the way of the gate between the two walls, by the king's garden, and the Chaldeans were around the city. And they went in the direction of the Aravah.

5 But the army of the Chaldeans pursued the king and overtook him in the plains of Yerikho, and all his army was scattered from him.

6 Then they captured the king and brought him up to the king of Babylon at Rivlah, and they passed sentence on him.

7 They slaughtered the sons of Tzidkiyahu before his eyes, and put out the eyes of Tzidkiyahu and bound him in chains and took him to Babylon.

8 In the fifth month, on the seventh day of the month — that was the nineteenth year of King Nevukhadnetzar, king of Babylon — Nevuzaradan, the captain of the bodyguard, a servant of the king of Babylon, came to Yerushalayim.

9 And he burned the house of YHWH and the king's house and all the houses of Yerushalayim — every great house he burned down.

10 And all the army of the Chaldeans, who were with the captain of the guard, broke down the walls around Yerushalayim.

11 And the rest of the people who were left in the city and the deserters who had deserted to the king of Babylon, together with the rest of the multitude, Nevuzaradan the captain of the guard carried into exile.

12 But the captain of the guard left some of the poorest of the land to be vinedressers and plowmen.

13 And the pillars of bronze that were in the house of YHWH, and the stands and the bronze sea that were in the house of YHWH, the Chaldeans broke in pieces and carried the bronze to Babylon.

14 And they took away the pots and the shovels and the snuffers and the dishes for incense and all the vessels of bronze used in the temple service,

15 The fire pans also and the bowls. What was of gold the captain of the guard took away as gold, and what was of silver, as silver.

16 The two pillars, the one sea, and the stands that Shelomoh had made for the house of YHWH, the bronze of all these vessels was beyond weight.

17 The height of the one pillar was eighteen cubits, and on it was a capital of bronze. The height of the capital was three cubits. A latticework and pomegranates, all of bronze, were all around the capital. And the second pillar had the same, with the latticework.

18 And the captain of the guard took Serayah the chief priest and Tzefanyahu the second priest and the three keepers of the threshold,

19 And from the city he took an officer who had been in command of the men of war, and five men of the king's council who were found in the city, and the secretary of the commander of the army who mustered the people of the land, and sixty men of the people of the land who were found in the city.

20 And Nevuzaradan the captain of the guard took them and brought them to the king of Babylon at Rivlah.

21 And the king of Babylon struck them down and put them to death at Rivlah in the land of Khamath. So Yehudah was taken into exile out of its land.

22 And over the people who remained in the land of Yehudah, whom Nevukhadnetzar king of Babylon had left, he appointed Gedalyahu the son of Akhikam, son of Shafan, governor.

23 Now when all the captains of the forces in the open country and their men heard that the king of Babylon had appointed Gedalyahu governor, they came with their men to Gedalyahu at Mitzpah — Yishma'el the son of Netanyah, and Yokhanan the son of Kare'akh, and Serayah the son of Tanhumeth the Netofathite, and Ya'azanyahu the son of the Ma'akhathite.

24 And Gedalyahu swore to them and their men, saying, Do not be afraid because of the Chaldean officials. Live in the land and serve the king of Babylon, and it will be well with you.

25 But in the seventh month, Yishma'el the son of Netanyah, son of Elishama, of the royal family, came with ten men and struck down Gedalyahu and put him to death along with the Yehudim and the Chaldeans who were with him at Mitzpah.

26 Then all the people, both small and great, and the captains of the forces arose and went to Egypt, for they were afraid of the Chaldeans.

27 And in the thirty-seventh year of the exile of Yeho'yakhin king of Yehudah, in the twelfth month, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, Evil-Merodakh king of Babylon, in the year that he began to reign, graciously freed Yeho'yakhin king of Yehudah from prison.

28 And he spoke kindly to him and gave him a seat above the seats of the kings who were with him in Babylon.

29 So Yeho'yakhin put off his prison garments. And every day of his life he dined regularly at the king's table.

30 And for his allowance, a regular daily allowance was given him by the king, according to his daily needs, all the days of his life.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Bet survives. Chapter 25 closes the covenant history of the monarchy with the destruction of the temple, the burning of Yerushalayim, the exile of the people, and the assassination of Gedalyahu. The final three verses — the release of Yeho'yakhin from prison thirty-seven years after the exile — are the covenant candle at the end of the darkness: the Davidic line is alive, honored, dining at the king's table.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 25 in full. Verse 9 — he burned the house of YHWH and the king's house and all the houses of Yerushalayim. The temple that YHWH filled with his glory at Shelomoh's dedication — the cloud too thick for the priests to stand — is now ash. The covenant presence that YHWH promised would be there always — Chapter 9:3 — departed before the fire came. Yehezkel's vision of the glory departing the temple preceded its physical destruction.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 27-30 — the final four verses of Melakhim are not lament but covenant hope in miniature. Yeho'yakhin is released. He is treated kindly. He sits above other kings. He dines regularly at the royal table. He receives a daily allowance all the days of his life. The Davidic covenant lamp — promised in Melakhim Aleph 11:36 — still burns in Babylon.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — they slaughtered the sons of Tzidkiyahu before his eyes, and put out the eyes of Tzidkiyahu and bound him in chains and took him to Babylon. The last thing Tzidkiyahu saw was the death of his sons. Then darkness. The king who rebelled against Babylon is carried to Babylon blind. Verse 13-16 — the two pillars Yakhin and Bo'az — broken in pieces and carried to Babylon as bronze. The covenant declaration at the temple entrance — he establishes, in him is strength — is dismantled into raw material for Babylon. Verse 27-30 — yet Yeho'yakhin is released and honored. The covenant with Dawid is not terminated. The lamp continues. Melakhim ends not with the fire of Yerushalayim but with a Davidic king eating at the table of the king of Babylon — alive, daily provision, all the days of his life. The covenant word of 2 Shemu'el 7:16 — your house and your kingdom shall be made sure forever — has not been canceled.

END OF PART FOUR — CHAPTERS 20 THROUGH 25 — MELAKHIM BET COMPLETE
